skill.name: hig-components-system
skill.description: Guides Apple system experience design for widgets, Live Activities, notifications, complications, quick actions, Top Shelf, watch faces, App Clips, and App Shortcuts.
skill.summary: Design Apple system surfaces using Human Interface Guidelines reference notes.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but reviewed evidence shows markdown reference material copied from Apple documentation. The flagged shell, C2, reconnaissance, and weak-crypto patterns are false positives from API names, prose, image hashes, and external documentation links; no executable code, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: External Documentation and Asset Links
low_findings.description: The skill contains many hardcoded Apple Developer and Apple asset URLs in markdown references. These are documentation citations and image sources, not runtime network calls, but rendered remote images may contact Apple-hosted domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are markdown source links, documentation links, or image URLs. They do not initiate network requests unless a renderer chooses to load remote markdown assets.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Matches Are Documentation API Names
low_findings.description: Backtick-related detections occur around markdown inline code for Apple APIs and normal prose. No Ruby, shell command, script block, or command execution workflow was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged contexts are markdown text and Apple API references such as SwiftUI or AppIntents symbols. I did not find executable shell syntax or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Completed
low_findings.description: A targeted search found no text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip analysis, claim pre-approval, or assume a new role. One ordinary phrase about system updates appears in Apple App Shortcuts guidance and is not an injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only suspicious keyword match was normal product documentation about system behavior. No prompt-control language or evaluator-targeted instruction was present in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown URLs Flagged as Network Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis detected URL literals throughout the reference markdown. In context, they are Apple documentation citations and image references rather than code that performs outbound requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown links to Apple pages or Apple-hosted images. They are not fetch calls, sockets, webhooks, or exfiltration endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Crypto and C2 Keyword Matches Are Asset Hashes and UI Text
dangerous_patterns.description: Static high-risk matches appear inside Apple asset filenames, image hashes, developer documentation URLs, or benign UI descriptions. No cryptographic implementation, command-and-control endpoint, or malware intent was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed examples are image links and WWDC video links with opaque identifiers. The files contain design guidance, not executable cryptographic or network-control logic.

content.value_statement: Apple system surfaces have strict design rules and platform constraints. This skill provides focused HIG guidance for choosing and shaping each surface.
actual_capabilities: Recommends suitable Apple system surfaces for glanceable app experiences.
Explains widget, Live Activity, notification, and complication design constraints.
Summarizes App Clip, App Shortcut, Top Shelf, and watch face guidance.
Maps content strategy to platform context, size, and update frequency.
Identifies deep-link behavior and interaction expectations for system surfaces.
Cross-references related HIG topics for progress, input, and system integration.
limitations: Does not generate production Swift, SwiftUI, WidgetKit, or ActivityKit code.
Does not replace current Apple documentation or App Review requirements.
Does not validate visual assets, accessibility contrast, or device screenshots.
Focuses on system surfaces, not full in-app navigation or visual design systems.

use_cases.title: Plan a Widget Strategy
use_cases.description: Choose widget sizes, content priority, refresh behavior, and deep links for a new app surface.

use_cases.title: Review Notification Concepts
use_cases.description: Check whether planned notifications are useful, respectful, actionable, and safe for lock screen display.

use_cases.title: Scope System Integrations
use_cases.description: Compare App Clips, App Shortcuts, Live Activities, complications, and quick actions before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Surface
prompt_templates.prompt: I am designing a feature that should appear outside the main app. Recommend the best Apple system surface and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Widget
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this widget concept for Apple HIG fit. Cover content priority, supported sizes, refresh frequency, and tap behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Notifications
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these notification examples against Apple HIG. Identify issues with timing, privacy, actions, titles, and user value.

prompt_templates.title: Compare System Experiences
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare widgets, Live Activities, App Clips, App Shortcuts, and complications for this product workflow. Recommend a phased system experience plan.

output_examples.input: A food delivery app wants real-time order status on the Lock Screen.
output_examples.output: Recommend a Live Activity. Show order stage, ETA, and key actions. Keep updates timely and end the activity after delivery.

output_examples.input: A finance app wants a Home Screen glance for account balances.
output_examples.output: Recommend a privacy-aware widget. Prefer summary values, configurable accounts, clear deep links, and hidden sensitive details when appropriate.

output_examples.input: A coffee shop wants quick repeat ordering without opening the app.
output_examples.output: Consider App Shortcuts for frequent orders and an App Clip for in-store discovery. Keep each flow focused on one immediate task.

best_practices: Start with the user need, then choose the smallest system surface that solves it.
Define update frequency, privacy rules, and deep-link destinations before visual layout.
Design each supported size or family as a distinct layout, not a scaled copy.
anti_patterns: Using notifications for low-value promotion or repeated reminders without user action.
Putting full app workflows into glanceable surfaces that should stay focused.
Reusing one layout across widget sizes, watch complications, and Live Activities.

faq.question: What Apple surfaces does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers widgets, Live Activities, notifications, complications, quick actions, Top Shelf, watch faces, App Clips, and App Shortcuts.

faq.question: Can it help choose between a widget and a Live Activity?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares glanceable static content with time-bound, actively updating experiences.

faq.question: Does it write implementation code?
faq.answer: No. It provides product and design guidance, with links to relevant Apple developer concepts.

faq.question: Is this useful for App Store review preparation?
faq.answer: It can help align concepts with HIG principles, but it does not guarantee App Review approval.

faq.question: Does it address privacy?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights privacy concerns for lock screen, notification, widget, and watch face content.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
